cue: How do you say someone lost their temper completely?

es.meaning: To fly into a rage; to hit the ceiling.
es.example: Cuando le dije que no, se subió a la parra.
es.exampleTranslation: When I said no, he hit the roof.
es.regionNote: Also carries a second sense of putting on airs. Reviewer to confirm which dominates in Mexico.

it.meaning: To fly into a rage; to lose all self-control.
it.example: Quando gli ho detto di no, è uscito dai gangheri.
it.exampleTranslation: When I said no, he went off the deep end.

bridge.note: Rage as displacement, in two directions. Spanish sends you UP — climbing the grapevine, out of reach, ranting from somewhere above the conversation. Italian sends you OFF — a door wrenched from its hinges, still recognisably itself but no longer attached to anything. Height versus detachment. And Italian keeps a spare: this is the third temper idiom in the bank, after the swelling nostrils and the fly on the nose.

interference.es_to_it: Grapevines carry no anger in Italian; the hinges do — uscire DAI gangheri.
interference.it_to_es: 'Salirse de los goznes' is not the Spanish idiom; anger climbs the parra.

review.notes: ES side discovered in the Spanish idioms book Joe is reading, 2026-07-21 (discovery only; all glosses and examples authored fresh). IT counterpart from Claude's recall — UNVERIFIED. Third temper entry — a themed run with 0085 is now possible.
